comment_text.sn6.3:1.3: This pair is found only here.
comment_text.sn6.3:3.3: For “presenting a regular oblation” (āhutiṁ niccaṁ paggaṇhāti) compare Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 1.9.2.19, where the sacrificer “seizes” (sampragṛhṇāti) the cup and spoon to make an oblation (āhuti). This is a householder “sacrifice with wife” (patnīsaṃyāja), where an oblation of ghee is offered to four pairs of gods with their wives for the sake of offspring. The lady of the house sits to the south-west of the fire. Here, however, she appears to be the chief sponsor.
comment_text.sn6.3:4.3: See Chāndogya Upaniṣad 4.3.7, where Brahmā is said to be the most magnificent because he eats what is not food.
comment_text.sn6.3:4.4: The “path to divinity” (brahmapatha) is said to be for one who sees the Self in the eye as the immortal Divinity. One such, even should they have no proper funeral service, travels after death along the path of light where they are received by a non-human who leads them to Divinity, from which there is no return (Chāndogya Upaniṣad 4.15).
comment_text.sn6.3:6.1: Here the familiar terms for recollection of the Saṅgha were deliberately repurposed from the Vedic meanings. Āhuneyyo is “worthy of offerings (āhuti) dedicated to the gods”.
comment_text.sn6.3:6.2: Dakkhiṇeyyo means “worthy of a religious donation”, the regular fee payable to priests for their services.
comment_text.sn6.3:7.3: Creatures “firm and frail” (tasathāvare) is a common Jain idiom (eg. Uttarādhyayana 5.8: tasesu thāvaresu ya, Sūyagaḍa 3.4.20: je keī tasathāvarā, Dasaveyāliya 4.1.42: tasaṁ vā thāvaraṁ vā, Ācāraṅgasūtra 9.1.14tasa-jīvā ya thāvarattāe).
comment_text.sn6.3:7.4: Commentary explains aggapiṇḍa as “alms for the senior Saṅgha”, in line with AN 4.34:7.1.
comment_text.sn6.3:10.1: According to the commentary, this verse was added by redactors.

root_text.sn6.3:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn6.3:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena aññatarissā brāhmaṇiyā brahmadevo nāma putto bhagavato santike agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajito hoti.
root_text.sn6.3:2.1: Atha kho āyasmā brahmadevo eko vūpakaṭṭho appamatto ātāpī pahitatto viharanto nacirasseva—yassatthāya kulaputtā sammadeva agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajanti, tadanuttaraṁ—brahmacariyapariyosānaṁ diṭṭheva dhamme sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja vihāsi.
root_text.sn6.3:2.2: “Khīṇā jāti, vusitaṁ brahmacariyaṁ, kataṁ karaṇīyaṁ, nāparaṁ itthattāyā”ti abbhaññāsi.
root_text.sn6.3:2.3: Aññataro ca panāyasmā brahmadevo arahataṁ ahosi.
root_text.sn6.3:3.1: Atha kho āyasmā brahmadevo pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya sāvatthiṁ piṇḍāya pāvisi.
root_text.sn6.3:3.2: Sāvatthiyaṁ sapadānaṁ piṇḍāya caramāno yena sakamātu nivesanaṁ tenupasaṅkami.
root_text.sn6.3:3.3: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmato brahmadevassa mātā brāhmaṇī brahmuno āhutiṁ niccaṁ paggaṇhāti.
root_text.sn6.3:3.5: “ayaṁ kho āyasmato brahmadevassa mātā brāhmaṇī brahmuno āhutiṁ niccaṁ paggaṇhāti.
root_text.sn6.3:3.7: Atha kho brahmā sahampati—seyyathāpi nāma balavā puriso samiñjitaṁ vā bāhaṁ pasāreyya, pasāritaṁ vā bāhaṁ samiñjeyya; evameva—brahmaloke antarahito āyasmato brahmadevassa mātu nivesane pāturahosi.
root_text.sn6.3:3.8: Atha kho brahmā sahampati vehāsaṁ ṭhito āyasmato brahmadevassa mātaraṁ brāhmaṇiṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn6.3:5.4: Yo te so piṇḍāya gharaṁ paviṭṭho.

translation_text.sn6.3:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn6.3:1.3: Now at that time a certain brahmin lady had a son called Brahmadeva, who had gone forth from the lay life to homelessness in the presence of the Buddha.
translation_text.sn6.3:2.1: Then Venerable Brahmadeva, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.
translation_text.sn6.3:2.2: He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.”
translation_text.sn6.3:2.3: And Venerable Brahmadeva became one of the perfected.
translation_text.sn6.3:3.1: Then Brahmadeva robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms.
translation_text.sn6.3:3.2: Wandering indiscriminately for almsfood in Sāvatthī, he approached his own mother’s home.
translation_text.sn6.3:3.3: Now at that time Brahmadeva’s mother, the brahmin lady, was presenting a regular oblation to the Divinity.
translation_text.sn6.3:3.5: “This Venerable Brahmadeva’s mother, the brahmin lady, offers up a regular oblation to the Divinity.
translation_text.sn6.3:3.6: Why don’t I go and stir up a sense of urgency in her?”
translation_text.sn6.3:3.7: Then, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he vanished from the realm of divinity and reappeared in the home of Brahmadeva’s mother.
translation_text.sn6.3:3.8: Then the divinity Sahampati, while standing in the air, addressed Brahmadeva’s mother in verse:
translation_text.sn6.3:4.1: “Far from here is the realm of divinity, madam,
translation_text.sn6.3:4.2: to which you present a regular oblation.
translation_text.sn6.3:4.3: But the Divinity doesn’t eat that kind of food.
translation_text.sn6.3:4.4: Why pray, when you don’t know the path to divinity?
translation_text.sn6.3:5.2: free of attachments, has surpassed the gods.
translation_text.sn6.3:5.3: Owning nothing, providing for no other, <j>a mendicant
translation_text.sn6.3:5.4: has entered your house for alms.
translation_text.sn6.3:6.1: He’s worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, <j>a knowledge master, evolved.
translation_text.sn6.3:6.2: He’s worthy of a religious donation <j>from gods and men.
translation_text.sn6.3:6.3: Having banished all evils, he’s unsullied.
translation_text.sn6.3:6.4: Cool at heart, he wanders searching for food.
translation_text.sn6.3:7.1: He has no before and after,
translation_text.sn6.3:7.2: peaceful, unclouded, untroubled, <j>with no need for hope,
translation_text.sn6.3:7.3: he has laid down the rod <j>for all creatures firm and frail.
translation_text.sn6.3:7.4: So let him enjoy your offering of alms for the highest.
translation_text.sn6.3:8.1: With peaceful mind, he has left the crowd,
translation_text.sn6.3:8.2: he wanders like a tamed elephant, unperturbed.
translation_text.sn6.3:8.3: He’s a mendicant fair in ethics, with heart well freed.
translation_text.sn6.3:8.4: So let him enjoy your offering of alms for the highest.
translation_text.sn6.3:9.2: present your religious donation <j>to one who is worthy of it.
translation_text.sn6.3:9.3: Now that you’ve seen the sage who has crossed over, <j>madam,
translation_text.sn6.3:9.4: make merit for the sake of future happiness!”
translation_text.sn6.3:10.2: she presented her religious donation <j>to one who is worthy of it.
translation_text.sn6.3:10.3: After seeing the sage who had crossed over, <j>the brahmin lady
translation_text.sn6.3:10.4: made merit for the sake of future happiness.

variant_text.sn6.3:5.4: so → te so (pts1ed); yo te sa (?)
